title: A $565 insurance saving in the regions could sting rich households
full_text:
Insurers have backed a NSW Treasury plan that would save households in flood-prone regions $565 a year in premiums by increasing charges on the top 5 per cent of land by value, including residential land worth more than $1.95 million.
The state parliament is considering five options to change the emergency services levy (ESL) proposed by the NSW Treasury to shift from a surcharge on insurance, which can contribute to underinsurance, to a general land tax.
A damaged house in Taree after floods in May 2025. Nine News
NSW is the last mainland state relying on an insurance-based levy, which adds up to 18 per cent on top of household insurance premiums and up to 34 per cent for businesses.
In an April discussion paper, NSW Treasury proposed five options to replace it: two based on fixed charges with discounts for the regions or surcharges for certain property types; and three based on fixed costs with tiers by land value.
All models propose that the majority of households will be better off, with an estimated $65 a year saving on average per household.
In its submission, the Insurance Council of Australia backed a six-tier model, in which households in the cheapest quarter by land value pay $141 a year, and those in the top 5 per cent pay $623. This option charges households with the highest property values $50 to $100 more a year than other options.
Levies for the ICA-backed option range from $175 to $17,972 for commercial properties; $275 to $50,857 for industrial properties; and $375 to $3280 for farms.
According to ICA-commissioned analysis by Lateral Economics, households in disaster-prone regional areas where premiums sit well above the state average would save about $565 a year; pensioner households with home, contents and motor vehicle cover would save about $125 a year; and strata households would save between $91 and $136 a year.
Small and medium businesses would see the largest savings, with representative examples from $105 a year for a small bakery; to $8850 for a restaurant; and more than $21,000 for a small livestock farm, it said.
ICA chief executive Andrew Hall said “the case for reform is overwhelming, and a fairer, simpler, and more equitable funding model is now well within reach”.
“No fair tax system should punish households and businesses for protecting their assets, least of all those who can least afford to pay.”
Business NSW chief executive Daniel Hunter said insurance has been the number one concern of small and medium business since 2019, and put pressure on the NSW Government to enact the reform within 12 months.
In the issues paper, NSW Treasury said about 55 per cent of insured properties in NSW would have incurred a lower levy under the replacement levy compared to the ESL.
“However, some changes in levy burden are unavoidable under a more community-based system that balances equity and simplicity,” it said.
“This will mean some property owners with properties of similar land values may pay more or pay less compared to the current ESL depending on where the property is located, property type, exposure to risk or level of insurance.”
YXJ0aWNsZToxZWFjZGQ4YS01ZmNlLTRmMDEtOWY5ZS1kZjlkYzhmYTRhNjY
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Karp
heading: General Insurance
